articles.title: A two-step tilt compensation method for off-axis holographic displays
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 06 August 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-55232-2 A two-step tilt compensation method for off-axis holographic displays
articles.content:
Abstract

Accurate diffraction between non-parallel planes is essential for holographic displays employing tilted spatial light modulators (SLMs). However, applying the standard angular spectrum method (ASM) directly to a tilted plane leads to geometric distortion and spectral aliasing. We present a two-step propagation method and its validation by experiments. The proposed method combines the standard ASM with a spatial-domain rotational transformation. The target image is first back-propagated to a parallel intermediate plane, then geometrically remapped onto the tilted SLM with phase correction. Implemented on an off-axis holographic display with \(30^\circ\) and \(45^\circ\) tilted SLM which leads to a large steering angle between the incident and reflected beam, the method is evaluated with multi-depth and multi-angle reconstructions. Results show improved image fidelity, stable depth performance, and robust compensation of off-axis holographic displays.

articles.title: HALO-GNN: hallucination-resistant temporal graph neural networks for dynamic community detection
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-60579-7 HALO-GNN: hallucination-resistant temporal graph neural networks for dynamic community detection
articles.content:
Abstract

Temporal graph learning is inherently subject to illusory structural dynamics, which is a phenomenon in which transient noise and ephemeral perturbations are exaggerated into deceptive community movements. Consequently, this leads to community assignments that are unstable and inconsistent, which severely limits the effectiveness of temporal graph neural networks in situations that involve streaming and dynamic circumstances. We provide a learning paradigm that is resistant to hallucinations and stabilizes temporal representations through the use of memory-guided structural regularization. The framework that has been suggested stabilizes node embeddings by referencing historical structures. This helps to reduce oscillations that are brought on by high-frequency noise while also preserving low-frequency development that is compatible with the community. Thorough evaluations across a variety of temporal graph benchmarks demonstrate significant improvements in robustness, temporal consistency, and perturbation resilience. These findings highlight the importance of hallucination resistance for the purpose of achieving reliable dynamic community detection.

articles.title: Bird swarm optimized chaotic lattice framework for parallel multi image encryption
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-59919-4 Bird swarm optimized chaotic lattice framework for parallel multi image encryption
articles.content:
Abstract

The exponential growth of multimedia communication demands secure, efficient, and scalable image encryption frameworks capable of handling high-dimensional correlated data. Existing chaotic and hybrid encryption schemes demonstrate strong nonlinearity but often suffer from redundant computations, weak inter-channel coupling, and limited adaptability in multi-image settings. In this work, we propose BLINK (Bird Swarm Optimization (BSO)-Hypertuned Lattice-Based Image eNcryption Kernel), a novel entropy-preserving multi-image encryption framework integrating chaotic lattice dynamics, eigen-coupled channel interaction, and optimization-driven parameter tuning. The proposed approach introduces a PSI-based amortized eigen-coupled chaotic lattice mechanism that enables efficient reuse of invariant chaotic structures while maintaining non-deterministic behavior. BSO is employed for adaptive hyperparameter tuning and secure seed generation, with cryptographic safeguards ensuring randomness quality. Furthermore, the framework aligns with post-quantum security principles through lattice-based constructs inspired by learning with errors. Experimental evaluations demonstrate superior entropy, reduced pixel correlation, and enhanced resistance to statistical and differential attacks compared to existing schemes, making BLINK suitable for secure multi-image encrypted transmission.

articles.title: Effectiveness of minimally invasive pre-measured endotracheal tube suction on physiological indicators in mechanically ventilated infants: a clinical trial study
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-60771-9 Effectiveness of minimally invasive pre-measured endotracheal tube suction on physiological indicators in mechanically ventilated infants: a clinical trial study
articles.content:
Abstract

Incorrect suctioning can have several adverse effects. This study aimed to determine the effectiveness of minimally invasive pre-measured endotracheal tube suctioning on physiological indicators in mechanically ventilated infants. In this crossover clinical trial study, 40 term infants admitted tithe NICU of Amirkola Children’s Hospital were randomly allocated to two groups with 20 infants in each group. In group A, infants first received pre-measured suction for 12 h, then routine suction for the next 12 h, and in group B, infants first received routine suction and then underwent pre-measured suction. Physiological indicators related to one minute before, during, one, two, five and ten minutes after suction and the time to return to baseline were recorded in the two groups. The mean differences in blood oxygen saturation (%) between the two suction methods at various time points were as follows: During suction, −1.95% (95% CI −2.99 to −0.90); 1 min post-suction, −2.25% (95% CI: −3.29 to − 1.20); 2 min, −2.42% (95% CI −3.43 to − 1.41); 5 min, −2.15% (95% CI −3.23 to −1.06); and 10 min, −2.35% (95% CI −3.18 to − 1.52) (P < 0.001). The time required for physiological parameters to return to baseline before suctioning was shorter with the pre-measured method compared with the routine method (P < 0.05). The fluctuations of physiological indicators, especially blood oxygen saturation, in the pre-measured suction method were lower than the routine method.

articles.title: Prevalence and related factors of Dupuytren’s contracture in a community-dwelling Japanese population
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-61045-0 Prevalence and related factors of Dupuytren’s contracture in a community-dwelling Japanese population
articles.content:
Abstract

Dupuytren’s contracture (DC) is a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia with substantial variation in prevalence across populations. However, epidemiological data from Asian countries remain limited, and little is known about upper-limb–specific quality of life or dietary correlates associated with DC. This cross-sectional study investigated the prevalence of DC in a community-dwelling Japanese population, assessed its impact on upper-limb function, and examined demographic, medical, lifestyle, and dietary correlates. DC diagnosis and severity were evaluated by hand surgeons using the original 5-grade clinical staging system proposed by Meyerding in 1936, a historical but practical method for epidemiological surveys that categorizes disease severity based on the number of affected digits and degree of flexion deformity. Demographic and lifestyle data were collected using standardized questionnaires, biochemical parameters were measured from fasting blood samples, upper-limb disability was assessed using the Quick Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (qDASH), and nutrient intake during the preceding month was estimated using the validated Brief Diet History Questionnaire (BDHQ). Among 1,304 participants, DC was identified in 100 (7.7%), with most cases classified as grade 0 (palpable nodules without digital flexion contracture). In univariate analyses, older age, male sex, smoking, diabetes mellitus, manual labor, and several nutrient intakes—including alcohol, protein, carbohydrate, sodium, and omega-3 fatty acids—were associated with DC. Multivariate analyses showed that older age, male sex, and alcohol intake were positively associated with DC. An inverse association between total energy intake and DC was also observed. No measurable difference in qDASH scores was observed according to the presence or severity of DC in this screening cohort. These findings provide additional epidemiological data on DC in a community-dwelling Japanese population, in which most detected cases were mild and identified at an early stage. The BDHQ-based dietary analyses were secondary and exploratory. Although alcohol intake was positively associated with DC, the dietary findings, including the inverse association with total energy intake, should be regarded as hypothesis-generating and require confirmation in longitudinal studies.

articles.title: The deubiquitinase USP25 contributes to stemness and malignant progression of breast cancer by stabilizing C1ql4
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-49272-x The deubiquitinase USP25 contributes to stemness and malignant progression of breast cancer by stabilizing C1ql4
articles.content:
Abstract

Breast cancer (BC) remains one of the most aggressive and life-threatening types of female cancer. Cancer stem cells (CSCs) are closely correlated with the progression and metastasis of cancers. This study aimed to explore the role of ubiquitin-specific peptidase 25 (USP25) in breast cancer metastasis and stemness. Cell counting kit 8 (CCK-8) and 5-ethynyl-2’-deoxyuridine (EDU) assay were performed to measure cell viability and proliferation. Xenograft tumor model was established to determine in vivo growth of cancer cells. Flow cytometry was used to measure cell apoptosis. Western blot and qPCR were performed to assess the expression of apoptosis and cancer stemness biomarkers. Cancer cell self-renewal ability was analyzed by the sphere formation assay. Clinical samples were collected to measure the expression of USP25 and C1ql4 (C1q-like 4). Knockdown of USP25 suppressed the in vitro and in vivo growth of breast cancer cells and increased cell apoptosis, inhibited the self-renewal ability, downregulated the expression of cancer stemness biomarkers, and reduced the stability of C1ql4 protein, whereas overexpression of C1ql4 could reverse these effects. The clinical analysis demonstrated that USP25 and C1ql4 were highly expressed in breast cancer tissues and presented a positive correlation. Our data indicated that knockdown of USP25 suppressed the stemness growth of breast cancer cells via reducing the stability of C1ql4 protein. These findings provide USP25/C1ql4 as a potential therapeutic target for breast cancer.

Introduction

Breast cancer (BC) remains one of the most aggressive and life-threatening types of cancer among women1. Despite advancements in early detection methods and treatment protocols, breast cancer can be aggressive and may recur or metastasize2. Noteworthy, there exists a special portion of cells named cancer stem cells (CSCs) within tumors, which share key traits with stem cells, including differentiation capabilities, self-renewal, and the expression of genes associated with stemness3,4. Their presence is strongly correlated with various aspects of tumor behavior such as progression, metastasis, drug resistance, and recurrence5,6. Understanding mechanisms that drive metastasis and cancer stemness in breast cancer is crucial7. It could pave the way for discovering new targets for therapies that specifically combat these processes, ultimately offering better prognostic outcomes for affected individuals.

Ubiquitination is a critical post-translational modification where a ubiquitin polypeptide is attached to proteins, either individually or as a chain, affecting various aspects such as protein activity, stability, localization, interactions between proteins, and their proteolysis by the 26 S proteasome6,8. This process plays a significant role in regulating numerous cellular functions7,8. Accumulating evidence suggests that post-translational modification of transcriptional regulators plays a crucial role for the modulation of fundamental biological behaviors such as cell growth, differentiation, and inflammatory response8,9,10. Ubiquitin-specific peptidase 25 (USP25) is a deubiquitinating enzyme and has been reported to exhibit pleiotropic effects on tumorigenesis and immune response9,11. Nevertheless, the function of USP25 in breast cancer progression has not been identified.

C1ql (C1q-like) serves as the initiating molecule for the classical complement pathway, recognizing immune complexes and initiating the complement system’s classical pathways12. Four members of the C1ql have been recognized exhibit high conservation across evolution, which contains the C1ql1 (CTRP14), C1ql2 (CTRP10), C1ql3 (CTRP13), and C1ql4 (CTRP11)13,14. Research indicates that C1ql1 plays a role as a novel promoter for glioma development, facilitating the conversion of neural stem cell-like cells into glial cells. This transformation is considered an early biomarker of malignant cell transition15. A recent work demonstrated high level of C1ql4 in breast cancer cell lines and clinical tissues, which is correlated with the malignancy of patients with breast cancer16.

In this study, we explored the role of USP25 in malignant progression and stemness of breast cancer and determined the potential mechanisms underlying its function.

Results

USP25 promotes the malignant phenotype of breast cancer cells in vitro

To analyze the effect of USP25 on breast cancer, MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells were transfected with USP25 siRNAs. The depletion efficiency was validated in the cells (Fig. 1A), and USP25 siRNA-1 was used in the subsequent analysis. The cell viability and Edu-positive cells were decreased by USP25 knockdown in MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells (Fig. 1B and C). MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cell apoptosis promoted by USP25 siRNA (Fig. 1D). The protein levels of Bcl-2 were downregulated and Bax and cleaved caspase-3 levels were upregulated by USP25 depletion in the cells (Fig. 1E). The sphere formation ability of MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells was inhibited by USP25 knockdown (Fig. 1F) and the levels of stemness biomarker, including ALDH1, and OCT4, were suppressed by the treatment of USP25 siRNA (Fig. 1G and H). Transwell invasion assays showed that knockdown of USP25 markedly impaired the invasive ability of MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells(Fig. 1I). The protein levels of EMT (Epithelial–mesenchymal transition)-related markers were significantly decreased in the USP25-knockout group (Fig. 1J). These data indicated that USP25 promotes the malignant phenotype of breast cancer cells in vitro.

USP25 contributes to the tumor growth of breast cancer in vivo

Next, we analyzed the role of USP25 in the tumor growth of breast cancer cells in nude mice. We observed that the depletion of USP25 could repress tumor volume and weight of breast cancer in the mice (Fig. 2A). The levels of USP25 and Ki-67 were inhibited by USP25 knockdown in the tumors (Fig. 2B). The protein levels of Bcl-2 were decreased and Bax and cleaved caspase-3 levels were increased by USP25 inhibition (Fig. 2C). The levels of stemness biomarker, including ALDH1, and OCT4, were suppressed by the USP25 depletion in the tumors (Fig. 2D and E). Western blot analysis showed that the expression levels of cell cycle-related proteins were significantly decreased after USP25 knockdown (Fig. 2F).

USP25 is required for stability of C1ql4 in breast cancer cells

Next, co-IP assays identified the interaction of USP25 with C1ql4 MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells (Fig. 3A). The expression of C1ql4 was decreased by USP25 siRNAs in MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells (Fig. 3B). The levels of C1ql4 were suppressed by USP25 depletion under the treatment of cycloheximide (CHX) at a time-dependent manner in MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells (Fig. 3C), suggesting that USP25 is required for stability of C1ql4 in breast cancer cells. The mRNA levels of USP25 and C1ql4 were measured using patient tissue samples obtained from the Affiliated Hospital of Chengde Medical University. lThese were increased in clinical breast cancer tissues (n = 50) relative to adjacent non-tumor tissues (n = 50) (Fig. 3D and E) and indicated a positive correlation in clinical breast cancer tissues (n = 50) (Fig. 3F). Besides, Co-IP assays demonstrated that C1ql4 ubiquitination was significantly elevated after USP25 knockdown in BT549 and MDA-MB-231 cells(Fig. 3G).

USP25 enhances malignant phenotypes of breast cancer cells by C1ql4

Subsequently, we examined whether C1ql4 was involved in USP25-mediated breast cancer progression. The protein levels of C1ql4 were inhibited by USP25 depletion, while C1ql4 overexpression rescued the levels in MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells (Fig. 4A). The knockdown of USP25 suppressed proliferation and stemness, and enhanced apoptosis of MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells, in which C1ql4 could reverse the phenotypes in the cells (Fig. 4B-G), as indicated by apoptosis biomarkers, sphere formation ability, and stemness biomarkers. To verify the respective effects of USP25 and C1ql4 on EMT and the cell cycle, we examined the expression of the relevant proteins and found that USP25 knockdown enhanced the epithelial characteristics of breast cancer cells and attenuated cell proliferation, whereas C1ql4 rescued these effects (Fig. 4H). Consistently, tumor growth of MDA-MB-231 cells in nude mice was inhibited by USP25 depletion and C1ql4 overexpression rescued the phenotypes in the model (Fig. 5). These data indicated that USP25 contributed to the malignant phenotypes of breast cancer cells by C1ql4.

Discussion

Cancer stem cells (CSCs) possess the features of high proliferation, self-renewal, and multidirectional differentiation, which have been indicated to be involved in the initiation, development, metastasis, and recurrence of cancers17,18. Cancer stem cells usually exhibit the stem cell features and participate in metastasis of cancers owing to its migration and invasion ability to distant organs19,20. Increasing studies have suggested that the gene expression pattern of the initiating cancer cells at the early metastasis stage is like that of stem cells19,20.

Some previous studies have shown that USP25 participates in multiple pathological processes and can regulate tumor progression in a context-dependent manner through stabilization of distinct substrates21,22,23,24,25,26,27,28.However, its role in breast cancer stemness and malignant behavior has remained unclear.

In the present research, we identified USP25 as a positive regulator of breast cancer stemness and malignant progression. Consistently, silencing USP25 also inhibited xenograft growth in vivo and reduced the expression of stemness-associated markers. These findings indicate that the high expression of USP25 contributes to maintaining the aggressive and stem-like phenotype of breast cancer cells.

Mechanistically, our research suggest that USP25 exerts these effects through C1ql4. We found that USP25 interacted with C1ql4, and USP25 depletion reduced C1ql4 protein abundance, shortened its stability under CHX treatment, and increased its ubiquitination level. In addition, USP25 and C1ql4 expression was positively correlated in clinical breast cancer tissues. More importantly, C1ql4 overexpression largely rescued the inhibitory effects of USP25 knockdown on proliferation, stemness, EMT-associated changes, and tumor growth. These results support that USP25 promotes breast cancer progression by stabilizing C1ql4. Our previous work showed that C1ql4 promotes breast cancer stemness and EMT through PI3K/AKT/NF-κB signaling16. Combined with our findings, it is possible that USP25-mediated stabilization of C1ql4 contributes to downstream oncogenic signaling involved in CSC maintenance. However, the present study did not address the upstream or downstream pathways associated with USP25 regulation. While our findings establish USP25 as a driver of breast cancer stemness and malignant progression, the precise signaling circuitry and tumor microenvironmental determinants governing its expression remain unresolved. In particular, whether hypoxia, chronic inflammatory signaling, or other microenvironment-derived cues contribute to USP25 upregulation in breast cancer warrants further investigation. Future studies should therefore systematically delineate how these extrinsic factors regulate USP25 expression in the breast cancer context.

Overall, our findings identify USP25 as a previously unrecognized regulator of breast cancer stemness and malignant progression and suggest that targeting the USP25/C1ql4 axis may represent a potential therapeutic strategy.

Materials and methods

Cell culture and treatment

Breast cancer cell lines MDA-MB-231 (ATCC HTB-26, Cellosaurus: CVCL_0062) and BT549 (ATCC HTB-122, Cellosaurus: CVCL_1092) were purchased from Shanghai Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and maintained in DMEM (Hyclone, USA) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; BI, China) and 1% streptomycin/penicillin (Sigma, USA) in a 37 °C incubator filled with 5% CO2.

Cell transfection and treatment

The siRNA targets the USP25 gene, and the negative control siRNA (Sequences: sense, 5′-UUCUCCGAACGUGUCACGUdTdT-3′; antisense, 5′-ACGUGACACGUUCGGAGAAdTdT-3′) were purchased from RiboBio (Guangzhou, China). The C1ql4 overexpression vectors were constructed using pcDNA3.1 as a backbone. The siRNAs and overexpression vectors were transfected into cancer cells using Lipofectamine RNAiMAX reagent (Invitrogen, USA) and PEI reagent (Sigma, USA), respectively. The cells were transfected for 48 h and treated with the cycloheximide (CHX, 200 ng/mL) for 24 h to suppress protein synthesis.

Quantitative real-time PCR

Total RNA was extracted from cells using Trizol reagent (Thermo, USA) and reverse transcribed to cDNA by using First-strand synthesis kit (Transgene, China). RNA level was measured using a SYBR Green Supermix (Transgene) and detected using the Real-Time PCR Detection System (Bio-Rad, USA). The β-Actin level was set as internal reference for normalization. USP25 primers (5′–3′): forward, 5′‑GTTCTATGGCAGATTCCTGGCTG‑3′; reverse, 5′‑GCAGCTTCTAGGCACTCATGCA‑3′.

Cell viability

Cell viability and proliferation were measured by cell counting kit 8 (CCK-8) and 5-ethynyl-2′-deoxyuridine (EDU) assay. For the CCK-8 assay, cells were incubated in 96-well plates and hatched for 24, 48, and 72 h. After reaction with CCK-8 reagent (Beyotime) for 2 h, the absorbance values at 450 nm were detected by a microplate spectrometer (Thermo, USA). For Edu assay, cells were fixed and permeabilized, and incubated with Edu reagent (Beyotime) and DAPI reagent (Sigma, USA). The images were captured using fluorescence microscopy (Leica, Germany).

Apoptosis detection

Cell apoptosis was assessed by using flow cytometry. The cells were incubated with propidium iodide (PI) and Annexin V (Beyotime, China) for 30 min and analyzed with the flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA).

Western blotting assay

Total proteins were harvested after homogenizing the cells with RIPA lysis buffer (SolarBio, China) and centrifuged at 12,000 g for 10 min. The proteins were separated with SDS-PAGE and transferred to a PVDF membrane (Millipore, Germany). Followed by blocking in skimmed milk. Protein bands were then probed with primary anti-USP25, anti-Bcl-2, anti-Bax, anti-Caspase-3, anti-ALDH1, anti-OCT4, anti-C1ql4, anti-β-actin overnight at 4 °C. Next day, the blots were incubated with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated secondary anti-mouse or anti-rabbit antibodies and reacted with ECL reagent (Millipore). Images were visualized in an imaging system (Tanon, China).

Sphere formation assay

Cells were suspended as single cells in serum-free DMEM/F12 medium (BI) that added with 20 ng/mL b-FGF (Sigma), 20 ng/mL EGF (Sigma), and 2% B27 (Sigma). Then, 5,000 cells were seeded into each well of the ultra-low attachment 96-well plate (Corning, USA) and incubated for 10 days at the 37 °C incubator. The images of mamo spheres were taken under a microscope (Leica, Germany).

Immunoprecipitation assays

The interaction of USP25 and C1ql4 was analyzed by co-immunoprecipitation (Co-IP) in the MDA-MB-231 and BT549 cells transfected with flag-USP25. Co-IPs were conducted by Pierce Co-Immunoprecipitation Kit (Fisher Scientific, Schwerte, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

Xenograft model

Animal experiments were authorized by the Ethics Committee of the Affiliated Hospital of Chengde Medical University. Female athymic nude mice (4–6 weeks old; 18–22 g; SiPeiFu, China) were housed under specific pathogen-free (SPF) conditions. In brief, MDA-MB-231 cells transfected by USP25 siRNAs, siNC, and C1ql4 overexpression vectors and collected in saline with 6 × 105 cells/100 µl. Then each mouse was hypodermically inoculated with 100 µl cell suspension in the right fat pad (n = 5 mice for each group). The width and length of the tumor were measured using vernier calliper every 5 days for 30 days. The tumor volume was calculated as width (mm)2 × length (mm)/2. All mice were finally sacrificed, and the tumors were weighed, photographed, and stored for analysis.

Immunohistochemistry (IHC) analysis

Tumor tissues were fixed in 10% neutral formalin (Sigma) for 3 days, dehydrated in serial ethanol, embedded with paraffine, and sliced into 5 μm thickness samples. The tissue samples were de-paraffinized and rehydrated. The endogenous peroxidase was blocked using 3% H2O2 at room temperature for 10 min and goat serum for 30 min. The tissue samples were then probed with anti-Ki-67 (Abcam, ab16667), anti-USP25 (Abcam, ab246948), and anti-C1ql4 (Affinity, DF9409) overnight at 4 °C. The samples were then incubated with HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies for 1 h. The samples were counterstained with hematoxylin, dehydrated, and mounted with a neutral balsam. Images were captured with a microscope (Leica).

Clinical samples

A total of 50 patients diagnosed with breast cancer and received surgical operation were recruited in this study and have signed informed consents. The tumor tissues and adjacent non-tumor tissues were resected during surgery and immediately frozen in liquid nitrogen, then stored at -80 °C refrigerator for subsequent experiments.

Statistics

Data are shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses are analyzed by using GraphPad Prism 7 software. Comparison between two groups and multiple groups was assessed using the unpaired two-tailed Student’s t test and one- or two-way analysis of variance followed by Tukey’s correction. A p-value less than 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant.

Animals and ethical statement

All methods were performed in accordance with the relevant guidelines and regulations. The animal study is reported in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines. Throughout the experiment, the health status of the mice was continuously monitored to prevent any unintended harm. Upon completion of all experimental procedures, the animals were humanely euthanized via cervical dislocation under isoflurane anesthesia, with tumor size strictly maintained below 13 mm in accordance with ethical guidelines. The carcasses were subsequently stored in a designated temporary freezer at the animal experiment center and later collected for standardized, environmentally safe disposal.

Conclusion

To conclude, we investigated the expression and functions of USP25 in breast cancer and demonstrated that knockdown of USP25 led to repressed proliferation and stemness of breast cancer cells, along with degradation of C1ql4. Overexpression of C1ql4 could reverse the effects of siUSP25. Our findings suggested that USP25/C1ql4 may be a promising therapeutic target for breast cancer metastasis.

articles.title: Joule-Heated direct writing: an electrically-driven additive manufacturing paradigm for space fabrication
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-46686-5 Joule-Heated direct writing: an electrically-driven additive manufacturing paradigm for space fabrication
articles.content:
Abstract

The imperative for in-space manufacturing drives the development of compact, energy-efficient metal additive manufacturing (AM) technologies. This work introduces and investigates a novel Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) process, inspired by the Joule–Lenz effect, for the direct writing of metallic structures. A fully coupled thermal-electrical-structural finite element model is developed to simulate the multilayer deposition of 304 stainless steel wire, elucidating the unique "tusk-shaped" temperature field evolution and the effects of current, speed, and contact width. Single-bead, five-layer walls are fabricated experimentally, and the influence of process parameters on macrostructure, geometry (width, height, overlap rate), surface roughness (Ra, Rz), and cross-sectional hardness is systematically characterized. The results demonstrate that optimal macroscopic quality is achieved at 120 A, 200 mm/min, and a 1.0 mm contact width. The forming width and overlap rate increase with current, while the forming height decreases; conversely, increasing speed reduces width and overlap but increases height. Surface roughness and hardness exhibit non-monotonic trends with varying parameters, with a minimum Ra of 0.142 µm attained. Simulated and experimentally measured voltages show good agreement, validating the multiphysics model.Validation results indicate a voltage discrepancy of less than 10% between simulation and experiment. The JHAM process, with its low power consumption (<1 kW) and simplified architecture, presents a promising candidate for on-orbit fabrication of metallic components.

Introduction

The advancement of long duration space exploration and the establishment of extraterrestrial habitats necessitate a paradigm shift from ground-based manufacturing to on-orbit, on-demand fabrication1,2,3,4. This transition is crucial in order to overcome the severe constraints of payload mass, volume, and associated launch costs. Additive manufacturing (AM) is widely recognized as a key enabling technology for this shift, as it offers the potential for in-situ repair, recycling, and production of strategic components5,6,7,8,9.

Global space agencies, including NASA, ESA, and the China Manned Space Agency, have active research programs focused on adapting AM for the space environment10,11. The overarching goal is to evolve the supporting infrastructure from “transporting everything” to "manufacturing what is needed" on-site12,13. Various studies have explored specific space AM applications, ranging from electron beam freeform fabrication and micro-droplet jetting to variable-gravity forming of polymers and space trusses14,15,16,17,18,19,20.

Current mainstream metal AM heat sources for terrestrial use—such as lasers21,22,23,24,25,26, arcs27,28,29,30,31,32, electron beams33,34,35,36,37,38, and plasma arcs39,40—present challenges for space applications, including high power demand, complex optics, and vacuum requirements. To address these limitations, we propose a Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) technique. Based on the fundamental Joule-Lenz law, JHAM utilizes the electrical resistance of the metal wire itself as the heat source for melting, offering inherent advantages of simplicity, low energy consumption, compactness, and gravity-independent operation.

Previous research on resistance-based AM has explored mechanisms of wire melting, droplet transfer, and single-layer formation. However, a comprehensive understanding of the multi-physics phenomena governing multi-layer deposition-encompassing dynamic heat generation, complex thermal history, and their resultant effects on geometry, surface integrity, and mechanical properties remains limited. Accurate numerical simulation of this process is challenging due to the moving contact zone and strong coupling between electrical, thermal, and mechanical fields.

Although our previous research41 demonstrated the feasibility of using Joule heating for single-layer droplet transfer, it did not address the cumulative thermal effects and structural stability required for 3D manufacturing. This study has made significant progress, focusing on the multi-layer deposition process and adopting a continuous roller structure as the in-situ hot rolling tool, as shown in Fig. 1

Experimental setup

Single-bead, five-layer walls were fabricated using 304 stainless steel wire (0.3 mm diameter). The upper electrode utilized a copper alloy roller (outer diameter: 12mm; inner diameter: 6mm; thickness: 8mm ). A constant mechanical contact force of 0.3 N was maintained throughout the deposition process using a spring-loaded tensioning system, ensuring both stable electrical conductivity and the aforementioned in-situ burnishing effect. While Table 4 lists the discrete parameter nodes utilized for the initial finite element simulation to establish the process window, the experimental validation explored a broader, continuous range of parameters to systematically capture the morphological evolution.The experimental setup consisted of a vacuum chamber, a wire feeding system, and a power supply, as shown in Fig. 2a. Surface roughness was measured using confocal laser scanning microscope (Keyence VK-X200, Japan) at the locations indicated in Fig. 2b.

Numerical simulation

A transient three-dimensional finite element model was developed, as shown in Fig. 3a, for simulating the layer-by-layer deposition process of five layers of metal wires. The meshing is shown in Fig. 3b. This process was decomposed into seven analysis steps: applying pressure, maintaining, applying current, roller movement (deposition), current removal, cooling, and pressure release (Fig. 3c). The model includes temperature-related material properties (Tables 1-3) and contact resistance. The material properties used in the simulation are listed in Tables 1, 2, and 3. The process parameters are detailed in Table 4.

To accurately capture the melting and solidification of the 304 stainless steel wire, the phase change process and the associated latent heat were incorporated using the equivalent heat capacity method. The latent heat of fusion (\(\text{L}=260 kJ/kg\)) is distributed over a solidus-liquidus temperature range (from \({\text{T}}_{\text{s}}=1400{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\) to \({\text{T}}_{\text{l}}=1450{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\)). This ensures that the energy balance during the solid-to-liquid transition is correctly conserved in the thermal field calculations.

The contact resistance at the roller-wire and wire-substrate interfaces plays a critical role in Joule heat generation. As shown in Table 3, the electrical contact resistance is modeled as a temperature-dependent variable. It progressively decreases as the local temperature increases, which physically reflects the material softening, the increase in actual contact area under constant pressure, and the eventual melting at the interface.

Given the simulated in-space vacuum environment, convective heat transfer is entirely neglected. Thermal dissipation is governed solely by thermal conduction within the solid structures and thermal radiation to the ambient environment. The radiation boundary condition is applied to all free surfaces governed by the Stefan-Boltzmann law, with an assumed ambient temperature of \(20{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\). The surface emissivity (\(\upvarepsilon\)) is set to \(0.4\) for the stainless steel and \(0.6\) for the copper roller. Mechanically, the bottom surface of the substrate is fully fixed, while a dynamic mechanical pressure is applied by the top roller.

To ensure numerical accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency, a mesh convergence test was conducted prior to the primary simulations. Three different mesh densities were evaluated for the deposition zone. The results indicated that refining the minimum element size below \(0.1 mm\) yielded a peak temperature variation of less than \(5\text{\%}\). Consequently, this optimal mesh density was adopted for the deposition region to capture the localized steep temperature gradients accurately.

Results and discussion

Temperature field evolution

The simulated temperature field evolution reveals a characteristic "tusk-shaped" profile (Fig. 2). During the roller movement step, the highest temperature region is not static; it initiates at the contact interface between the newest (fifth) and the previous (fourth) layer, then shifts rearward relative to the roller’s motion. The formation of this asymmetric profile is governed by competing heat transfer mechanisms: the copper roller acts as a significant heat sink, inducing rapid quenching at the contact front.Under vacuum conditions, the absence of convective heat transfer fundamentally alters the thermal dissipation characteristics. At the contact front, the copper roller serves as a primary heat sink, inducing rapid quenching. Conversely, heat dissipation at the rear is governed exclusively by thermal radiation and conduction through the built wall. This imbalance leads to a localized heat accumulation behind the moving roller, which is the primary driver for the asymmetric tusk-shaped temperature field.This directional heat flow helps stabilize the deposition by preventing the re-melting of previously solidified layers. The isothermal surfaces are dense beneath the roller due to rapid conduction and sparse behind it due to residual heat, creating the asymmetric, elongated profile. See Fig. 4

The ivory-like temperature field results from the increasing number of wire layers. When heat transfer and radiation are ignored, heat generation occurs only in the contact region beneath the roller, which moves with the roller (Fig. 5a).When heat transfer and radiation are considered but roller–substrate heat transfer is ignored, the temperature field trails behind the roller movement (Fig. 5b).When roller–substrate heat transfer is also considered (Fig. 5c), the larger roller volume causes region A to lose most of its heat to the roller, making it cooler than the heat-producing zone. Region C is warmer than region A due to combined prior and current heat production. Region B, despite similar combined heating, loses more heat to the roller than downward, making it cooler than region C. Region D, being closer to the heat source, is slightly warmer than region C. Thus, the temperature order is A < B < C < D, forming the ivory-like field in Fig. 5d.

A parametric study quantified the effects of current (I), speed (V), and contact width (W) on the peak temperature and melt zone extent (Fig. 6, Table 4). Within the studied range, every 10 A increase in current raised the peak temperature by approximately 300°C. Increasing the speed by 100 mm/min reduced the peak temperature by about 150°C, as the heating time per unit length decreased. Increasing the contact width by 0.2 mm reduced the peak temperature by roughly 200°C, due to a decrease in current density for a fixed current. Critically, the simulations identify parameter windows that achieve sufficient melting for bonding without excessive heat input that causes collapse or excessive remelting of lower layers.

Macrostructure and geometry analysis

Figure 7 presents the macroscopic top-view morphologies of the single-bead five-layer walls fabricated under varying process parameters (current: 100–140 A, speed: 100–300 mm/min, and section width: 0.6–1.4 mm). The results demonstrate that the optimal macroscopic quality, characterized by a flat surface devoid of deformation or gaps, is achieved at a current of 120 A, a speed of 200 mm/min, and a section width of 1.0 mm. Deviations from these optimal values led to significant degradation in forming quality. Specifically, fluctuations in current or section width altered the degree of wire melting, shifting the morphology from under-melted (poor adhesion) to over-melted (collapse). Similarly, variations in deposition speed disrupted the stability of the layer formation, resulting in inconsistent surface quality.

The impact of varying current, speed, and section width on the cross-sectional morphology, forming size, and overlap rate of single-bead multilayer walls is depicted in Fig. 8a, b, and c. Within a certain range, a gradual increase in current enhances the forming width of a single-bead multilayer wall while simultaneously reducing the forming height. However, the change in width is significantly less pronounced than that in height, and the overlap rate progressively increases due to the heightened heat production associated with larger currents.Physically, the increase in current leads to higher Joule heat generation (\(Q={I}^{2}Rt\)), which raises the melt pool temperature. This elevated temperature reduces the viscosity and surface tension of the molten metal, thereby enhancing its fluidity and wettability. Consequently, the molten metal spreads more readily across the substrate, resulting in a wider bead and a reduced layer height. On the other hand, increasing the speed significantly reduces the bonding rate, leading to a decrease in the forming width and an increase in the forming height. This shift is attributed to the reduction in linear heat input (Joules per mm) as speed increases. With less thermal energy delivered per unit length, the volume of the molten pool decreases, and the solidification rate accelerates. The molten metal solidifies before it can fully spread outwards, leading to a narrower width and a piled-up morphology. Additionally, increasing the section width solely increases the forming height, with both the forming width and overlap rate decreasing. Notably, a shift from a 0.9 mm to a 1.0 mm section width resulted in the most significant decrease in the overlap rate, decreasing by 38.71% compared to the preceding period. This decrease is due to the increased section width and decreased current density, leading to dispersed heat production and reduced wire melting.

Surface roughness and hardness analysis

The surface quality of the contact surface is crucial in JHAM, as it directly impacts heat production within the system. Fig. 9a, b, c, and 9d illustrate the measurement positions and results of surface roughness for a single-bead multilayer wall. Due to significant size variations on the side of the single-bead multilayer wall caused by the step effect, analyzing surface roughness on this surface is not critical. Consequently, measurements were conducted solely on the top surface, revealing that the surface roughness Ra could reach as low as 0.142 μm.It is critical to clarify that the minimum Ra of 0.142 μm reported here is measured strictly on the top surface. This exceptional smoothness is not a natural result of wire melting but is achieved through the in-situ hot-burnishing (ironing) effect. The copper roller applies continuous mechanical pressure to the semi-molten metal during deposition, flattening the surface irregularities before final solidification, effectively acting as an integrated finishing process. The side surfaces, however, naturally retain the step-effect typical of layer-by-layer manufacturing. Fig. 9e and f elucidate the influences of different process parameters on the surface roughness Ra and Rz of a single-bead multilayer wall, as well as the section hardness. Both Ra and Rz initially decrease and then increase with increasing process parameters across the three groups. Conversely, the section hardness exhibits a non-monotonic trend. Initially, the hardness increases as the process parameters optimize the layer density. However, with excessive heat input, the hardness demonstrates a declining trend. This reduction in hardness (softening) under high heat input is attributed to the grain coarsening effect, governed by the Hall-Petch relationship. Prolonged residence at elevated temperatures and slower cooling rates in the vacuum environment promote grain growth, which reduces the density of grain boundaries and consequently lowers the mechanical resistance. Although detailed EBSD analysis is beyond the scope of this work, the observed hardness trends are consistent with these thermally-driven microstructural changes. Future microstructural investigations are required to definitively correlate these hardness variations with grain size evolution via the Hall-Petch relationship.Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 9(i), the three-dimensional perspective surface scan of the single-bead multilayer wall exhibits a continuous and flat surface morphology without obvious macroscopic surface defects such as un-melted gaps or severe distortion. This macroscopic integrity further substantiates the technological feasibility of the JHAM process.

Model validation

A key validation of the multiphysics model is the comparison of the electrical response. Fig. 6h plots the simulated and experimentally measured voltage across the roller and substrate during a deposition run at I = 120 A, V = 200 mm/min. The simulated voltage remains stable at ~600 mV, reflecting the steady-state contact resistance in the model. The experimental voltage fluctuates around this value (e.g., 550-650 mV), which is expected due to minor variations in real-time contact pressure and surface conditions.The fidelity of the multiphysics model is corroborated by both electrical and geometric responses. While the average voltage error remains below 10%, a comparative analysis of the simulated melt pool dimensions and the experimental bead cross-sections (as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5) reveals a strong correlation in geometric trends. The model successfully predicts the expansion of the fusion zone with increasing current and its contraction with increasing speed, thereby validating its predictive capability for the JHAM process. Beyond the electrical response, the model’s fidelity is further corroborated by geometric trends. As demonstrated in both the simulated melt pool dimensions and the experimental cross-sections (Fig. 4 and Fig. 5), the predicted trends—where increasing current widens the fusion zone while increasing speed narrows it—show strong qualitative and quantitative agreement. This confirms that the model reliably captures the thermal-structural coupling during the deposition process.

Conclusion

This work presents a comprehensive investigation into Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) through integrated multiphysics simulation and experimental validation. The main conclusions are:

  • The numerical model successfully predicts the formation of a distinctive "tusk-shaped" temperature field in multi-layer deposition, arising from the dynamic balance between localized Joule heating, heat conduction to the roller, and accumulation in the track.

  • Process parameters (current, speed, contact width) critically control peak temperature, melt pool size, and consequently, the geometric accuracy of the built wall. Optimal macroscopic morphology for 304 stainless steel was achieved at 120 A, 200 mm/min, and 1.0 mm width.

  • Geometric analysis reveals that increasing current promotes width and inter-layer fusion (overlap) but reduces height; increasing speed reduces width and overlap but can increase height; increasing contact width increases height but reduces width and overlap.

  • Surface roughness (Ra, Rz) and cross-sectional micro-hardness show non-monotonic relationships with process parameters, indicating the existence of optimal windows for surface finish and mechanical properties.The close match between simulated and experimental voltage signals validates the fidelity of the coupled electrical-thermal model.

  • The JHAM process demonstrates compelling attributes-low power (<1 kW), simplicity, and gravity independence for in-space manufacturing applications. Future work will focus on multi-material capabilities, micro-structure control, and the fabrication of more complex 3D geometries.

articles.title: Differential effects of plyometric training loads on jump and sprint performance reveal optimal strategies
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-41245-4 Differential effects of plyometric training loads on jump and sprint performance reveal optimal strategies
articles.content:
Abstract

Plyometric training is an effective method for enhancing lower-body muscle power, and modulating external loads may further optimize its training effects. Currently, there is a lack of systematic comparisons between externally loaded and unloaded plyometric training with respect to performance outcomes, such as vertical jump, horizontal jump, and sprint performance. Moreover, the differential effects of various load intensities remain unclear. Therefore, this study employs a meta-analysis to systematically evaluate the effects of plyometric training under different loads on jump and sprint performance. Following PRISMA guidelines, randomized controlled trials published between January 2010 and April 2025 were systematically searched in PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, EBSCO, and CNKI databases. Inclusion criteria were: healthy participants, an experimental group performing externally loaded plyometric training, a control group performing unloaded plyometric training, and outcome measures including jump and/or sprint performance. Fourteen studies were ultimately included. Externally loaded training was categorized into four groups based on load intensity: assisted load (APT, < body weight), low load (LPT, ≤ 5% body weight), medium load (MPT, 5–15% body weight), and high load (HPT, > 15% body weight). A meta-analysis was conducted using RevMan 5.4 software to pool effect sizes, test heterogeneity, perform subgroup analyses, and generate forest plots. Data were synthesized using random-effects or fixed-effects models, with effect sizes expressed as standardized mean differences. The significance level was set at p < 0.05. Vertical Jump: Only MPT significantly improved performance vs. UPT (ES = 0.43, p < 0.0001). APT, LPT, and HPT showed no significant difference. Horizontal Jump: No significant differences found among any load intensities vs. UPT (ES = 0.09, p = 0.40). Sprint: LPT (ES=-0.92, p = 0.0005) and MPT (ES=-0.23, p = 0.04) significantly improved performance vs. UPT. HPT showed no significant effect. Medium-load (5–15% body weight) plyometric training is most effective for improving vertical jump performance. Both low-load and medium-load training are more effective than unloaded training in enhancing sprint performance. Assisted-load training yields effects similar to those of unloaded training on jump performance. No significant differences were observed among various load intensities in improving horizontal jump performance. It is recommended to select appropriate load intensities based on specific performance goals in practice and to avoid excessive loading (i.e., 15% of body weight) to maintain movement quality and training adaptability.
